title: T-Mobile bungled forced plan migration, canceling some users' free lines
gist: T-Mobile's forced migration of legacy customers to new rate plans erroneously stripped some longtime subscribers of free promotional lines (in one case pushing a bill from about $50 to over $300) and mis-billed Hulu add-ons, while separately imposing durable $6-per-line price hikes as it eliminates roughly 1,100 legacy billing codes to simplify its back-end systems.

themes.theme: T-Mobile legacy plan migration execution failures

insights.claim: T-Mobile is eliminating older rate plans and automatically migrating customers to new plans, a process that erroneously canceled some longtime customers' free-line promotions and added unexpected hotspot data charges of up to $15/month; T-Mobile publicly admitted the errors and says it is restoring discounts and backdating credits.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: A visible billing-system failure during a mass migration signals execution risk in T-Mobile's back-end simplification project; investors should watch for customer churn, FCC complaints, or class-action exposure similar to the 2024 price-lock backlash.
insights.quote: We've identified technical issues affecting a very small number of customers and are working quickly to correct them.

insights.claim: One Reddit user reported that after migration to the 'Experience Signature' plan, their bill jumped from about $50/month (three paid lines plus six free lines) to over $300, with the free lines dropped from the billing breakdown entirely; another customer reported a $200 bill increase from lost free-line promotions.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Illustrates the scale of potential billing errors (6x bill increase in one case) that could translate into a wave of support tickets, regulatory complaints, and reputational damage if not resolved quickly and comprehensively.
insights.quote: Just received my first bill today after being migrated to Experience Signature and bill is >$300. The bill breakdown did not include any free lines carried over.
insights.locator: Reddit post cited in the article

insights.claim: T-Mobile is also investigating separate reports that some customers were incorrectly billed for Hulu following the same plan migration.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: A second, independent billing defect (beyond free lines) surfacing in the same migration event suggests systemic quality-control gaps in the migration project rather than an isolated bug, raising the risk of further undiscovered errors.
insights.locator: We're also investigating reports that some people were incorrectly billed for Hulu

insights.claim: While T-Mobile is reversing the erroneous free-line cancellations, it is not reversing the up-to-$6-per-line-per-month price increases tied to the same migration, and previously refused to reverse a separate 2024 decision that broke its 'Un-carrier' lifetime price-guarantee promise, which is still the subject of a pending class-action lawsuit that T-Mobile is trying to push into arbitration.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: T-Mobile is willing to reverse clear billing bugs but not its intentional pricing decisions, indicating the $6/line increases are a durable, deliberate revenue action rather than a mistake — useful for modeling T-Mobile's postpaid ARPU trajectory and ongoing legal/regulatory liability from the 2024 price-lock litigation.
insights.locator: T-Mobile ended lifetime price guarantee in 2024

insights.claim: T-Mobile COO Jon Freier told staff in a leaked email that the company is removing about 1,100 legacy billing codes from its systems (leaving fewer than 100), that nearly half of migrated customers will see no price change, and that customers with a price change will see it capped at up to $6/line, with all migrated customers receiving a five-year price guarantee plus better data, hotspot, and international coverage.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scope of T-Mobile's back-end IT simplification (91%+ reduction in billing codes) — a legitimate cost/complexity-reduction driver that likely improves long-term margins and product velocity, but the botched rollout shows near-term execution and customer-retention risk during the transition.
insights.quote: Nearly half of these customers won't see their price change at all by the time this migration is complete. For those who do, It's up to $6 per line.
insights.locator: quote from T-Mobile COO Jon Freier's leaked email
